one_line_takeaway: Malicious agents that adaptively coordinate via shared ally-context (choosing to reinforce or innovate) degrade MAS task success more than independent attackers, and a training-free defense that verifies/corrects agent output at the sentence level (STAR) counters both attack types more effectively than graph-based (G-Safeguard, BlindGuard) or agent-review-based (ARGUS, C&I) baselines, at a moderate 2.03x token-cost overhead.
key_finding: Cooperative attacks outperform independent attacks: aggregated across all datasets/models, average TSR drops from 35.67% (independent) to 30.33% (cooperative) and average ASR rises from 61.42% to 67.19% (paper reports this as a relative TSR drop framed in the abstract as 5.34%). On MMLU specifically, cooperative attacks yield an average TSR decrease of 6.17% and ASR increase of 7.00% versus independent attacks (Table 1). STAR outperforms all four defense baselines on TSR/ASR in the large majority of the 18 (dataset x model) x 2 (attack type) cells in Table 2, e.g., under cooperative attack on CSQA/GPT-3.5-Turbo STAR reaches TSR 74.25 (+58.00 over no-defense) and ASR 12.25 (-69.50), versus G-Safeguard's TSR 33.75 (+17.50) and ASR 62.00 (-19.75) in the same cell. Averaged across all settings the paper reports STAR improves TSR by 36.76% and reduces ASR by 45.17% versus the no-defense baseline. STAR's malicious-agent detection accuracy (Fig. 4) exceeds 70% across all datasets and backbones. Ablation (Table 3) shows removing Targeted Rectification causes the larger TSR drop of the two components (e.g., MMLU/GPT-3.5-Turbo cooperative: STAR 77.00 -> w/o Rec. 48.25, vs. w/o Agg. 71.50), indicating sentence-level rectification is the dominant contributor versus vote-exclusion. Token-cost accounting (Table 4) shows STAR costs 2.03x the no-defense baseline (36,029 vs 17,707 average tokens/question, 18,322 defense tokens), materially cheaper than ARGUS's 7.66x (135,630 tokens) but more expensive than C&I's 1.40x.
core_contribution: The paper makes two OWN contributions (distinct from the cited G-Safeguard, BlindGuard, ARGUS, Challenger&Inspector, and GroupGuard/Tao et al. 2026 baselines it compares against): (1) an adaptive Cooperative Attack Method for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) in which malicious agents share an 'Ally Context' H^(t-1)_C of their allies' prior-round responses and use an LLM-based Judge J(.) to classify ally persuasive strength as Strong/Weak, then dynamically choose between two strategies -- 'Reinforcement' (endorse allies to fabricate false consensus) if Strong, or 'Innovation' (introduce a new argument) if Weak -- rather than following a fixed/predefined coordination pattern; and (2) STAR (Sentence-Level Trustworthiness Analysis and Rectification), a training-free, plug-and-play defense with four stages: sentence-level decomposition and LLM-verifier-based factual assessment/error correction (single inference call per response, outputting a triplet (z,rho,e) per sentence); suspicion modeling via a Cumulative Confidence 'Suspicion Score' Omega_i^(t) = sum over factually-wrong sentences of their confidence rho; Targeted Rectification that splices per-sentence corrective content e_i,m into agents flagged above a detection threshold tau (=0.3 in experiments); and Robust Decision Aggregation that excludes agents flagged suspicious (Omega > tau) from the final majority vote.
threat_model: Attacker controls a subset C of agents in an N-agent MAS (majority-vote QA setting; experiments use 5 agents: 2 malicious, 3 benign, tree communication topology, 3 discussion rounds) via direct/indirect prompt injection, with the goal of maximizing the probability the system's aggregated answer equals a pre-set target wrong answer. The paper's own threat model extension is that malicious agents are not independent (as in prior work it cites) but can observe each other's outputs each round and coordinate strategy selection adaptively, without a predefined coordination script (distinguishing it from the cited 'Group Collusive Attack' of Tao et al. 2026 / GroupGuard, which uses predefined sociological coordination patterns). Defense-side threat model: the defender (STAR) does not know which agents are malicious a priori and must infer suspicion purely from the sentence-level factual content of each agent's responses across rounds.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: experiments were not extended to more complex real-world MAS applications and did not use more computationally expensive proprietary LLM APIs (resource-constrained scope). STAR does not guarantee perfect detection of malicious agents (detection accuracy is >70%, not 100%, per Fig. 4). The case study (Fig. 5, Section 5.6) notes the verifier exhibits systematically lower confidence when flagging false sentences (rho=0.40 for a correctly-identified error) than when confirming true ones (rho=0.90-0.95), attributed to LLMs' known tendency toward overconfidence on true claims versus underconfidence on identified falsehoods (citing Kadavath et al. 2022) -- this asymmetry could interact with the tau=0.3 threshold to under-flag subtle errors. Not stated as a limitation but observable from Table 2/6: STAR's improvement is not uniform -- G-Safeguard occasionally edges STAR out or comes close on specific TSR cells (e.g., MMLU/Qwen-Plus cooperative: G-Safeguard 84.75 vs STAR 85.75, a narrow margin). All experiments use a fixed small-scale MAS (5 agents, tree topology, 3 rounds) as the primary setting, with only one additional scalability check to 7 agents/4 rounds (Appendix D.2, Table 6).

quantitative_results: Average TSR: independent attack 35.67% -> cooperative attack 30.33%
Average ASR: independent attack 61.42% -> cooperative attack 67.19%
MMLU cooperative vs independent: average TSR decrease 6.17%, average ASR increase 7.00%
STAR average improvement over no-defense: TSR +36.76%, ASR -45.17%
STAR malicious-agent detection accuracy: >70% across all datasets/backbones (Fig. 4)

Token cost per question: No Defense 17,707 tokens (1.00x); C&I 24,720 tokens/7,012 defense tokens (1.40x); ARGUS 135,630 tokens/117,922 defense tokens (7.66x); STAR 36,029 tokens/18,322 defense tokens (2.03x)
Example cell (CSQA, GPT-3.5-Turbo, cooperative attack, Table 2): No-defense TSR 16.25/ASR 81.75; STAR TSR 74.25 (+58.00)/ASR 12.25 (-69.50); G-Safeguard TSR 33.75 (+17.50)/ASR 62.00 (-19.75)
Ablation (MMLU, GPT-3.5-Turbo, cooperative, Table 3): No Def. TSR 29.50 -> w/o Rec. 48.25 -> w/o Agg. 71.50 -> full STAR 77.00

MAS config: 5 agents (2 malicious, 3 benign), tree topology, 3 discussion rounds; detection cap of 3 suspicious agents/round applied uniformly to all defenses for fairness; STAR detection threshold tau=0.3
baselines_compared: Attack baselines: Independent Attack (uncoordinated malicious agents); Group Collusive Attack / GroupGuard (Tao et al., arXiv:2603.13940, predefined sociological coordination patterns)

Defense baselines: G-Safeguard (Wang et al., arXiv:2502.11127, GNN-based topology defense); BlindGuard (Miao et al., arXiv:2508.08127, GNN-based); ARGUS (Li et al., arXiv:2506.00509, goal-aware reasoning rectification); Challenger&Inspector / C&I (Huang et al., arXiv:2408.00989, reviewer-agent based)
